data: Fragment of a list of names
A small triangular fragment of white marble, intact along the lower edge, but broken on the other sides. The reverse is cut flat, but only roughly finished.

Coarse-grained, homogeneous, white marble, likely Aphrodisian, Parian-2, Parian-3, Parian-4, Proconnesian, Thasian-3. Digital microscopy: indistinguishable fabric (identification based on pXRF, digital microscopy)
Preserves traces of 6 lines of Greek letters, with a clear vacat below. There are clear spaces between each word (name).

Moderately regular thinly incised letters with pronounced terminal serifs, and a fairly square module. Alpha with straight bar; delta (and sigma) show slight curvature; kappa with short arms; omicron slightly smaller (c.2mm less) and above the line; sigma with parallel outer strokes, and sometimes a pronounced extension to the mid-join; phi with extended vertical (11mm) and small over-semi-circular loops.
Purchased by the Siracusa Museum, as coming from Akrai, in 1888; now in storage, magazzino B, cass.6
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2014-09-11

An outstanding collection of archaeological material. The collection ranges from Siracusa and the surrounding territory (Megara Hyblaia, Akrai, Kasmenai etc) to encompass all of eastern Sicily, reaching as far as Gela and Agrigento. The ground floor covers prehistory through to the end of the Classical period. Upstairs includes Hellenistic and Roman Syracuse and a display of material from the catacombs. A gallery displaying material from Hellenistic eastern Sicily is in preparation.
Inscriptiones Graecae Siciliae et Italiae, additis graecis Galliae Hispaniae, Britanniae, Germaniae inscriptionibus

G. Kaibel, Inscriptiones Graecae Siciliae et Italiae, additis graecis Galliae Hispaniae, Britanniae, Germaniae inscriptionibus, Inscriptiones Graecae consilio et auctoritate Academiae Litterarum Regiae Borussicae Editae. Volumen XIV., XIV (Berlin: Georgius Reimerus, 1890)
Iscrizioni greche lapidarie del Museo di Palermo
M.T. Manni Piraino, Iscrizioni greche lapidarie del Museo di Palermo, Sikelika 6 (Palermo: S. F. Flaccovio, 1973)

Accademia nazionale dei Lincei, Istituto nazionale di archeologia e storia dell’arte (Italy), e Reale Accademia d’Italia, «Notizie degli scavi di antichità», Notizie degli Scavi di Antichità, 1876, http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1646037
G. Pugliese Carratelli, «Silloge dell’ epigrafi acrensi», in Akrai, a c. di L. Bernabó Brea (Catania, 1956), 151–81
Jonathan Prag revised from autopsy notes and photograph
Pugliese Carratelli, 1956, Silloge dell' epigrafi acrensi

Apparatus criticus

  • Text from autopsy;
  • 1: Not noted in previous editions
  • 3: Pugliese Carratelli: -].Σ ΦΙΛ[-
  • 4: Orsi, Kaibel: -]ΙΔΑΣ ΦΙΛ[-
  • 5: Orsi, Kaibel: -]ΝΙΔΑΣ ΗΡΑ[-; Pugliese Carratelli: -]Ι̣ΝΙΛΑΣ ΗΡΑ[-

translation

commentary

As noted already by Orsi (with Halbherr), this is a list of names. In lines 3-6, a name in the nominative is separated by one letter space from what is presumably the associated patronymic (in the genitive). In line 2, the letters ΝΤΟΣ are set somewhat further to the right than the names below, but a longer name in the nominative ending in -ντος seems the most likely resolution (since a singular genitive participle referring to e.g. office held, or activity undertaken hardly fits with a list of nominative names below). It is clear that the gap between names is in no way aligned from one line to the next. Kaibel included the text in the addenda to IG XIV, and there proposed that it should be connected to another fragment with nominative names, also from Akrai, and in Palermo museum (ISic001034), although he noted in his commentary that his description of the latter was clearly flawed, if this was the case. Pugliese Carratelli merely noted the proposal without judgement, but as Manni Piraino observes, the piece presented here is of marble, while the Palermo fragment is of the local limestone (Kaibel should have trusted his original notes!), and the two cannot be part of the same stone. A closer parallel from Akrai is offered by a fragment recording gymnasiarchs (ISic001033. Several similar marble fragments with names are preserved from Hellenistic Syracuse also. (Note that IGPalermo misreports the inventory number as 657.)
